title: A note on the internet after AI when clarity started to feel like rebellion
summary: An essay on the internet after AI, with one eye on the machinery and one eye on the people asked to live with it.
topic: the internet after AI when clarity started to feel like rebellion
body: There was a version of the internet after AI circulating in April 2026 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Standing inside a Sunday morning with fewer tabs open than usual, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of what keeps this topic interesting is that it resists clean ideological sorting. The internet after AI can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

In 2026, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not just adopting systems; they were auditioning identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted relief from tasks that had become exhausting, repetitive, or plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. It is that you end up building institutions around a feeling, and institutions are much harder to unwind than a hot take in a group chat.

Over time I have learned to pay close attention to what disappears from a story once people start repeating it professionally. Usually it is the same cluster of missing pieces: labor, error handling, emotional load, repair, governance, and the uneven distribution of inconvenience. The internet after AI looked different to me once I framed it that way. What had first appeared as a sleek solution started to read more like a bundle of trade-offs with a persuasive publicist. That does not make the thing useless. It just makes it legible again, which is often the first real service criticism can provide.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It lets me see not only what the technologies were doing, but what I was learning to notice. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. The internet after AI sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from April 2026: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: The problem with slow writing is usually what remains when convenience becomes ambient
summary: A reflection on slow writing and the kind of world it quietly assumes.
topic: slow writing while trying to keep a human scale
body: I wrote this down in April 2026 after thinking about slow writing from the vantage point of a small desk in good light. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. Slow writing seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, slow writing stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

Looking back from April 2026, I could already feel my own position shifting. A few months earlier I had written “What trustworthy interfaces taught me about building futures,” and the difference between that piece and this one is instructive. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. Here I was more interested in what happens after the applause: the maintenance burden, the political assumptions, the quiet social sorting, the emotional contract being drafted between human beings and the systems now arranging their choices. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was just incomplete in a way optimism often is when the machinery has not yet touched enough lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can admire good tools without surrendering the right to ask what world they are quietly assembling around themselves. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. It begins by refusing the oversized story until the smaller one proves it deserves to grow. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “The problem with safety debates is usually how novelty can impersonate depth.”

A final note to myself from April 2026: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: What trustworthy interfaces taught me about building futures
summary: A post about trustworthy interfaces after years of moving too quickly, written against the temptation to make the future sound cleaner than it is.
topic: trustworthy interfaces after years of moving too quickly
body: My notes on trustworthy interfaces began with a small irritation in March 2026. Everyone around me seemed to agree that the big story was obvious, and the agreement itself made me uneasy. When consensus arrives too quickly, I start looking for whatever got edited out to make the sentence so clean. In this case the omitted piece was the social cost of never pausing to notice what changed. That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

Trustworthy interfaces appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Every tool arrives carrying an image of the person who will use it and the society it hopes to stabilize. If you listen carefully, you can hear those assumptions in the design choices. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also what kind of behavior, dependency, and mood the system quietly normalizes while it works.

What I kept seeing was a gap between prototype conditions and public conditions. In the prototype, everyone knows they are participating in an experiment. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. It is another to fold the same logic into schools, offices, hospitals, transit, or housing and expect gratitude because the interface looks modern. The social cost of never pausing to notice what changed becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

What changed most was not my taste for the topic but my threshold for abstraction. After writing “On online identity when the novelty tax has mostly been paid,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. If a claim about the future cannot survive contact with somebody's day, their tools, their fatigue, and their incentives, I do not trust the claim to survive for long at all. That has become one of my small private tests. Ask the sentence to descend from altitude. If it lands gracefully, keep it. If it panics on impact, start over.

What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. But let us stay attentive to the conditions under which usefulness turns into dependence and strangeness gets marketed as destiny. Trustworthy interfaces seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. More often it is a style of relationship that becomes normal before we have finished deciding whether we like it.

A final note to myself from March 2026: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. When consensus arrives too quickly, I start looking for whatever got edited out to make the sentence so clean. In this case the omitted piece was what remains when convenience becomes ambient. That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

Model reliability appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. title: The problem with digital twins is usually what happens when a product grows more competent than legible
summary: An essay on digital twins, with one eye on the machinery and one eye on the people asked to live with it.
topic: digital twins once maintenance became the real product
body: There was a version of digital twins circulating in December 2025 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Standing inside a train ride where the future looked mostly like error handling, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of what keeps this topic interesting is that it resists clean ideological sorting. Digital twins can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

In 2025, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not just adopting systems; they were auditioning identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted relief from tasks that had become exhausting, repetitive, or plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. It is that you end up building institutions around a feeling, and institutions are much harder to unwind than a hot take in a group chat.

Over time I have learned to pay close attention to what disappears from a story once people start repeating it professionally. Usually it is the same cluster of missing pieces: labor, error handling, emotional load, repair, governance, and the uneven distribution of inconvenience. Digital twins looked different to me once I framed it that way. What had first appeared as a sleek solution started to read more like a bundle of trade-offs with a persuasive publicist. That does not make the thing useless. It just makes it legible again, which is often the first real service criticism can provide.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It lets me see not only what the technologies were doing, but what I was learning to notice. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Digital twins sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from December 2025: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

body: There was a version of local inference circulating in September 2025 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Standing inside an office where the exciting part was no longer the model but the glue, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of what keeps this topic interesting is that it resists clean ideological sorting. Local inference can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

In 2025, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not just adopting systems; they were auditioning identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted relief from tasks that had become exhausting, repetitive, or plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. title: What agents taught me about building futures

topic: agents once maintenance became the real product
body: I wrote this down in September 2025 after thinking about agents from the vantage point of a train ride where the future looked mostly like error handling. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. Agents seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, agents stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

I kept thinking about an older post of mine, “The problem with interface trust is usually the strange politics of delegating judgment,” from August 2025. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. It begins by refusing the oversized story until the smaller one proves it deserves to grow. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “On open weights as the internet learned a new kind of performance.”

A final note to myself from September 2025: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

In 2025, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not just adopting systems; they were auditioning identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted relief from tasks that had become exhausting, repetitive, or plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. It is that you end up building institutions around a feeling, and institutions are much harder to unwind than a hot take in a group chat.

Over time I have learned to pay close attention to what disappears from a story once people start repeating it professionally. Usually it is the same cluster of missing pieces: labor, error handling, emotional load, repair, governance, and the uneven distribution of inconvenience. Workflow automation looked different to me once I framed it that way. What had first appeared as a sleek solution started to read more like a bundle of trade-offs with a persuasive publicist. That does not make the thing useless. It just makes it legible again, which is often the first real service criticism can provide.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It lets me see not only what the technologies were doing, but what I was learning to notice. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Workflow automation sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from June 2025: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. title: The problem with evidence chains is usually the strange politics of delegating judgment
summary: A personal essay about evidence chains, and the longer argument hidden inside the strange politics of delegating judgment.
topic: evidence chains when reliability turned out to be the whole game
body: I wrote this down in June 2025 after thinking about evidence chains from the vantage point of an office where the exciting part was no longer the model but the glue. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. Evidence chains seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, evidence chains stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

Looking back from June 2025, I could already feel my own position shifting. A few months earlier I had written “The problem with evaluation is usually what happens when a product grows more competent than legible,” and the difference between that piece and this one is instructive. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. Here I was more interested in what happens after the applause: the maintenance burden, the political assumptions, the quiet social sorting, the emotional contract being drafted between human beings and the systems now arranging their choices. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was just incomplete in a way optimism often is when the machinery has not yet touched enough lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can admire good tools without surrendering the right to ask what world they are quietly assembling around themselves. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. It begins by refusing the oversized story until the smaller one proves it deserves to grow. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “What research theater taught me about building futures.”

A final note to myself from June 2025: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: The problem with evaluation is usually what happens when a product grows more competent than legible
summary: An essay on evaluation, with one eye on the machinery and one eye on the people asked to live with it.
topic: evaluation once maintenance became the real product
body: My notes on evaluation began with a small irritation in June 2025. Everyone around me seemed to agree that the big story was obvious, and the agreement itself made me uneasy. When consensus arrives too quickly, I start looking for whatever got edited out to make the sentence so clean. In this case the omitted piece was what happens when a product grows more competent than legible. That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

Evaluation appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Every tool arrives carrying an image of the person who will use it and the society it hopes to stabilize. If you listen carefully, you can hear those assumptions in the design choices. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also what kind of behavior, dependency, and mood the system quietly normalizes while it works.

What I kept seeing was a gap between prototype conditions and public conditions. In the prototype, everyone knows they are participating in an experiment. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. It is another to fold the same logic into schools, offices, hospitals, transit, or housing and expect gratitude because the interface looks modern. What happens when a product grows more competent than legible becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

What changed most was not my taste for the topic but my threshold for abstraction. After writing “On interface trust after the magic show,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. If a claim about the future cannot survive contact with somebody's day, their tools, their fatigue, and their incentives, I do not trust the claim to survive for long at all. Evaluation seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. title: The problem with operator tooling is usually the strange politics of delegating judgment
summary: A reflection on operator tooling and the kind of world it quietly assumes.
topic: operator tooling when reliability turned out to be the whole game
body: My notes on operator tooling began with a small irritation in March 2025. Everyone around me seemed to agree that the big story was obvious, and the agreement itself made me uneasy. When consensus arrives too quickly, I start looking for whatever got edited out to make the sentence so clean. In this case the omitted piece was the strange politics of delegating judgment. That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

Operator tooling appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Every tool arrives carrying an image of the person who will use it and the society it hopes to stabilize. If you listen carefully, you can hear those assumptions in the design choices. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also what kind of behavior, dependency, and mood the system quietly normalizes while it works.

What I kept seeing was a gap between prototype conditions and public conditions. In the prototype, everyone knows they are participating in an experiment. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. It is another to fold the same logic into schools, offices, hospitals, transit, or housing and expect gratitude because the interface looks modern. The strange politics of delegating judgment becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

What changed most was not my taste for the topic but my threshold for abstraction. After writing “What evaluation taught me about building futures,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. If a claim about the future cannot survive contact with somebody's day, their tools, their fatigue, and their incentives, I do not trust the claim to survive for long at all. That has become one of my small private tests. Ask the sentence to descend from altitude. If it lands gracefully, keep it. If it panics on impact, start over.

What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. But let us stay attentive to the conditions under which usefulness turns into dependence and strangeness gets marketed as destiny. Operator tooling seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. More often it is a style of relationship that becomes normal before we have finished deciding whether we like it.

A final note to myself from March 2025: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: What workflow automation taught me about building futures
summary: A post about workflow automation once maintenance became the real product, written against the temptation to make the future sound cleaner than it is.
topic: workflow automation once maintenance became the real product
body: There was a version of workflow automation circulating in March 2025 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Standing inside a train ride where the future looked mostly like error handling, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of what keeps this topic interesting is that it resists clean ideological sorting. Workflow automation can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

In 2025, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not just adopting systems; they were auditioning identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted relief from tasks that had become exhausting, repetitive, or plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. It is that you end up building institutions around a feeling, and institutions are much harder to unwind than a hot take in a group chat.

Over time I have learned to pay close attention to what disappears from a story once people start repeating it professionally. Usually it is the same cluster of missing pieces: labor, error handling, emotional load, repair, governance, and the uneven distribution of inconvenience. Workflow automation looked different to me once I framed it that way. What had first appeared as a sleek solution started to read more like a bundle of trade-offs with a persuasive publicist. That does not make the thing useless. It just makes it legible again, which is often the first real service criticism can provide.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It lets me see not only what the technologies were doing, but what I was learning to notice. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Workflow automation sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from March 2025: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: On evidence chains after the magic show
summary: A personal essay about evidence chains, and the longer argument hidden inside the long argument between autonomy and auditability.
topic: evidence chains after the magic show
body: I wrote this down in February 2025 after thinking about evidence chains from the vantage point of a desktop crowded with small useful automations. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. Evidence chains seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, evidence chains stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

Looking back from February 2025, I could already feel my own position shifting. A few months earlier I had written “What evaluation taught me about building futures,” and the difference between that piece and this one is instructive. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. Here I was more interested in what happens after the applause: the maintenance burden, the political assumptions, the quiet social sorting, the emotional contract being drafted between human beings and the systems now arranging their choices. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was just incomplete in a way optimism often is when the machinery has not yet touched enough lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can admire good tools without surrendering the right to ask what world they are quietly assembling around themselves. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. It begins by refusing the oversized story until the smaller one proves it deserves to grow. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “The problem with coordination systems is usually software stepping into spaces that used to belong to habit.”

A final note to myself from February 2025: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: The problem with agents is usually the difference between an assistant and a dependency
summary: A personal essay about agents, and the longer argument hidden inside the difference between an assistant and a dependency.
topic: agents once maintenance became the real product
body: There was a version of agents circulating in November 2024 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Standing inside a desktop crowded with small useful automations, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of what keeps this topic interesting is that it resists clean ideological sorting. Agents can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

In 2024, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not just adopting systems; they were auditioning identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted relief from tasks that had become exhausting, repetitive, or plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. It is that you end up building institutions around a feeling, and institutions are much harder to unwind than a hot take in a group chat.

Over time I have learned to pay close attention to what disappears from a story once people start repeating it professionally. Usually it is the same cluster of missing pieces: labor, error handling, emotional load, repair, governance, and the uneven distribution of inconvenience. Agents looked different to me once I framed it that way. What had first appeared as a sleek solution started to read more like a bundle of trade-offs with a persuasive publicist. That does not make the thing useless. It just makes it legible again, which is often the first real service criticism can provide.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It lets me see not only what the technologies were doing, but what I was learning to notice. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Agents sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from November 2024: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. title: What ambient assistants taught me about building futures
summary: An essay on ambient assistants, with one eye on the machinery and one eye on the people asked to live with it.
topic: ambient assistants after the magic show
body: I wrote this down in November 2024 after thinking about ambient assistants from the vantage point of a morning spent comparing outputs instead of admiring them. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. Ambient assistants seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, ambient assistants stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

Looking back from November 2024, I could already feel my own position shifting. I kept thinking about an older post of mine, “The problem with memory systems is usually the difference between an assistant and a dependency,” from September 2024. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. Here I was more interested in what happens after the applause: the maintenance burden, the political assumptions, the quiet social sorting, the emotional contract being drafted between human beings and the systems now arranging their choices. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was just incomplete in a way optimism often is when the machinery has not yet touched enough lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can admire good tools without surrendering the right to ask what world they are quietly assembling around themselves. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. It begins by refusing the oversized story until the smaller one proves it deserves to grow. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “I keep coming back to contactless interfaces.”

A final note to myself from November 2024: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: The problem with evaluation is usually the difference between an assistant and a dependency
summary: A reflection on evaluation and the kind of world it quietly assumes.
topic: evaluation once maintenance became the real product
body: I wrote this down in July 2024 after thinking about evaluation from the vantage point of a desktop crowded with small useful automations. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. Evaluation seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, evaluation stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

Looking back from July 2024, I could already feel my own position shifting. A few months earlier I had written “What local inference taught me about building futures,” and the difference between that piece and this one is instructive. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. Here I was more interested in what happens after the applause: the maintenance burden, the political assumptions, the quiet social sorting, the emotional contract being drafted between human beings and the systems now arranging their choices. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was just incomplete in a way optimism often is when the machinery has not yet touched enough lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can admire good tools without surrendering the right to ask what world they are quietly assembling around themselves. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. It begins by refusing the oversized story until the smaller one proves it deserves to grow. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “What warehouse software taught me about building futures.”

A final note to myself from July 2024: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. Local inference seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. title: The problem with workflow automation is usually the difference between an assistant and a dependency

body: My notes on workflow automation began with a small irritation in March 2024. Everyone around me seemed to agree that the big story was obvious, and the agreement itself made me uneasy. When consensus arrives too quickly, I start looking for whatever got edited out to make the sentence so clean. In this case the omitted piece was the difference between an assistant and a dependency. That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

Workflow automation appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Every tool arrives carrying an image of the person who will use it and the society it hopes to stabilize. If you listen carefully, you can hear those assumptions in the design choices. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also what kind of behavior, dependency, and mood the system quietly normalizes while it works.

In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. It is another to fold the same logic into schools, offices, hospitals, transit, or housing and expect gratitude because the interface looks modern. title: What evidence chains taught me about building futures
summary: An essay on evidence chains, with one eye on the machinery and one eye on the people asked to live with it.
topic: evidence chains after the magic show
body: There was a version of evidence chains circulating in March 2024 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Standing inside a morning spent comparing outputs instead of admiring them, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of what keeps this topic interesting is that it resists clean ideological sorting. Evidence chains can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

In 2024, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not just adopting systems; they were auditioning identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted relief from tasks that had become exhausting, repetitive, or plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. It is that you end up building institutions around a feeling, and institutions are much harder to unwind than a hot take in a group chat.

Over time I have learned to pay close attention to what disappears from a story once people start repeating it professionally. Usually it is the same cluster of missing pieces: labor, error handling, emotional load, repair, governance, and the uneven distribution of inconvenience. Evidence chains looked different to me once I framed it that way. What had first appeared as a sleek solution started to read more like a bundle of trade-offs with a persuasive publicist. That does not make the thing useless. It just makes it legible again, which is often the first real service criticism can provide.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It lets me see not only what the technologies were doing, but what I was learning to notice. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Evidence chains sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from March 2024: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: On evaluation while everyone tried to make software feel responsible
summary: A reflection on evaluation and the kind of world it quietly assumes.
topic: evaluation while everyone tried to make software feel responsible
body: I wrote this down in February 2024 after thinking about evaluation from the vantage point of a whiteboard full of edge cases rather than aspirations. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. Evaluation seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, evaluation stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

Looking back from February 2024, I could already feel my own position shifting. A few months earlier I had written “I keep coming back to local inference,” and the difference between that piece and this one is instructive. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. Here I was more interested in what happens after the applause: the maintenance burden, the political assumptions, the quiet social sorting, the emotional contract being drafted between human beings and the systems now arranging their choices. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was just incomplete in a way optimism often is when the machinery has not yet touched enough lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can admire good tools without surrendering the right to ask what world they are quietly assembling around themselves. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. It begins by refusing the oversized story until the smaller one proves it deserves to grow. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “The problem with delivery logistics is usually software stepping into spaces that used to belong to habit.”

A final note to myself from February 2024: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. title: The problem with chat interfaces is usually the pressure to form a worldview too quickly
summary: A reflection on chat interfaces and the kind of world it quietly assumes.
topic: chat interfaces before the story settled
body: There was a version of chat interfaces circulating in November 2023 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Standing inside an exhausted midnight walk after too much time online, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of what keeps this topic interesting is that it resists clean ideological sorting. Chat interfaces can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

In 2023, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not just adopting systems; they were auditioning identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted relief from tasks that had become exhausting, repetitive, or plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. It is that you end up building institutions around a feeling, and institutions are much harder to unwind than a hot take in a group chat.

Over time I have learned to pay close attention to what disappears from a story once people start repeating it professionally. Usually it is the same cluster of missing pieces: labor, error handling, emotional load, repair, governance, and the uneven distribution of inconvenience. Chat interfaces looked different to me once I framed it that way. What had first appeared as a sleek solution started to read more like a bundle of trade-offs with a persuasive publicist. That does not make the thing useless. It just makes it legible again, which is often the first real service criticism can provide.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It lets me see not only what the technologies were doing, but what I was learning to notice. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Chat interfaces sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from November 2023: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. title: What benchmarks taught me about building futures
summary: A post about benchmarks while taste and trust were both up for grabs, written against the temptation to make the future sound cleaner than it is.
topic: benchmarks while taste and trust were both up for grabs
body: I wrote this down in October 2023 after thinking about benchmarks from the vantage point of a coffee shop conversation that turned into an accidental seminar. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. Benchmarks seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, benchmarks stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

Looking back from October 2023, I could already feel my own position shifting. I kept thinking about an older post of mine, “The problem with AI search is usually the pressure to form a worldview too quickly,” from August 2023. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. Here I was more interested in what happens after the applause: the maintenance burden, the political assumptions, the quiet social sorting, the emotional contract being drafted between human beings and the systems now arranging their choices. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was just incomplete in a way optimism often is when the machinery has not yet touched enough lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can admire good tools without surrendering the right to ask what world they are quietly assembling around themselves. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. It begins by refusing the oversized story until the smaller one proves it deserves to grow. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “On telepresence rituals under pressure.”

A final note to myself from October 2023: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: On prompt rituals when everyone had a demo but nobody had a shared vocabulary

topic: prompt rituals when everyone had a demo but nobody had a shared vocabulary
body: My notes on prompt rituals began with a small irritation in October 2023. Everyone around me seemed to agree that the big story was obvious, and the agreement itself made me uneasy. When consensus arrives too quickly, I start looking for whatever got edited out to make the sentence so clean. In this case the omitted piece was what gets lost when the demo becomes the argument. That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. title: The problem with model demos is usually the pressure to form a worldview too quickly
summary: A personal essay about model demos, and the longer argument hidden inside the pressure to form a worldview too quickly.
topic: model demos before the story settled
body: I wrote this down in June 2023 after thinking about model demos from the vantage point of an exhausted midnight walk after too much time online. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. Model demos seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, model demos stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

Looking back from June 2023, I could already feel my own position shifting. A few months earlier I had written “What image models taught me about building futures,” and the difference between that piece and this one is instructive. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. Here I was more interested in what happens after the applause: the maintenance burden, the political assumptions, the quiet social sorting, the emotional contract being drafted between human beings and the systems now arranging their choices. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was just incomplete in a way optimism often is when the machinery has not yet touched enough lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can admire good tools without surrendering the right to ask what world they are quietly assembling around themselves. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. It begins by refusing the oversized story until the smaller one proves it deserves to grow. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “On coordination systems under pressure.”

A final note to myself from June 2023: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: What image models taught me about building futures
summary: An essay on image models, with one eye on the machinery and one eye on the people asked to live with it.
topic: image models while taste and trust were both up for grabs
body: My notes on image models began with a small irritation in May 2023. Everyone around me seemed to agree that the big story was obvious, and the agreement itself made me uneasy. When consensus arrives too quickly, I start looking for whatever got edited out to make the sentence so clean. In this case the omitted piece was the difference between surprise and understanding. That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

Image models appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Every tool arrives carrying an image of the person who will use it and the society it hopes to stabilize. If you listen carefully, you can hear those assumptions in the design choices. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also what kind of behavior, dependency, and mood the system quietly normalizes while it works.

What I kept seeing was a gap between prototype conditions and public conditions. In the prototype, everyone knows they are participating in an experiment. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. It is another to fold the same logic into schools, offices, hospitals, transit, or housing and expect gratitude because the interface looks modern. The difference between surprise and understanding becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

What changed most was not my taste for the topic but my threshold for abstraction. After writing “On AI search when everyone had a demo but nobody had a shared vocabulary,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. If a claim about the future cannot survive contact with somebody's day, their tools, their fatigue, and their incentives, I do not trust the claim to survive for long at all. That has become one of my small private tests. Ask the sentence to descend from altitude. If it lands gracefully, keep it. If it panics on impact, start over.

What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. But let us stay attentive to the conditions under which usefulness turns into dependence and strangeness gets marketed as destiny. Image models seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. More often it is a style of relationship that becomes normal before we have finished deciding whether we like it.

A final note to myself from May 2023: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: On chat interfaces when everyone had a demo but nobody had a shared vocabulary
summary: A reflection on chat interfaces and the kind of world it quietly assumes.
topic: chat interfaces when everyone had a demo but nobody had a shared vocabulary
body: There was a version of chat interfaces circulating in May 2023 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Standing inside a group chat melting down over the same screenshot, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of what keeps this topic interesting is that it resists clean ideological sorting. Chat interfaces can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

In 2023, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not just adopting systems; they were auditioning identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted relief from tasks that had become exhausting, repetitive, or plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. It is that you end up building institutions around a feeling, and institutions are much harder to unwind than a hot take in a group chat.

Over time I have learned to pay close attention to what disappears from a story once people start repeating it professionally. Usually it is the same cluster of missing pieces: labor, error handling, emotional load, repair, governance, and the uneven distribution of inconvenience. Chat interfaces looked different to me once I framed it that way. What had first appeared as a sleek solution started to read more like a bundle of trade-offs with a persuasive publicist. That does not make the thing useless. It just makes it legible again, which is often the first real service criticism can provide.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It lets me see not only what the technologies were doing, but what I was learning to notice. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Chat interfaces sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from May 2023: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.
