title: The problem with safety debates is usually the pressure to form a worldview too quickly
summary: A reflection on safety debates and the kind of world it quietly assumes.
topic: safety debates before the story settled
body: My notes on safety debates began with a small irritation in January 2023. Everyone around me seemed to agree that the big story was obvious, and the agreement itself made me uneasy. When consensus arrives too quickly, I start looking for whatever got edited out to make the sentence so clean. In this case the omitted piece was the pressure to form a worldview too quickly. That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

Safety debates appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Every tool arrives carrying an image of the person who will use it and the society it hopes to stabilize. If you listen carefully, you can hear those assumptions in the design choices. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also what kind of behavior, dependency, and mood the system quietly normalizes while it works.

What I kept seeing was a gap between prototype conditions and public conditions. In the prototype, everyone knows they are participating in an experiment. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. It is another to fold the same logic into schools, offices, hospitals, transit, or housing and expect gratitude because the interface looks modern. The pressure to form a worldview too quickly becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

What changed most was not my taste for the topic but my threshold for abstraction. After writing “I keep coming back to tool-using models,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. If a claim about the future cannot survive contact with somebody's day, their tools, their fatigue, and their incentives, I do not trust the claim to survive for long at all. That has become one of my small private tests. Ask the sentence to descend from altitude. If it lands gracefully, keep it. If it panics on impact, start over.

What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. But let us stay attentive to the conditions under which usefulness turns into dependence and strangeness gets marketed as destiny. Safety debates seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. body: There was a version of language models circulating in December 2022 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Standing inside a browser window full of generated images, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of what keeps this topic interesting is that it resists clean ideological sorting. Language models can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

In 2022, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not just adopting systems; they were auditioning identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted relief from tasks that had become exhausting, repetitive, or plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. title: On AI search as the internet learned a new kind of performance
summary: A personal essay about AI search, and the longer argument hidden inside the pressure to form a worldview too quickly.
topic: AI search as the internet learned a new kind of performance
body: I wrote this down in November 2022 after thinking about AI search from the vantage point of a quiet hour reading papers with too many grand nouns. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. AI search seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, AI search stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

Looking back from November 2022, I could already feel my own position shifting. A few months earlier I had written “I keep coming back to tool-using models,” and the difference between that piece and this one is instructive. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. Here I was more interested in what happens after the applause: the maintenance burden, the political assumptions, the quiet social sorting, the emotional contract being drafted between human beings and the systems now arranging their choices. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was just incomplete in a way optimism often is when the machinery has not yet touched enough lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can admire good tools without surrendering the right to ask what world they are quietly assembling around themselves. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. It begins by refusing the oversized story until the smaller one proves it deserves to grow. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “What homebound automation taught me about building futures.”

A final note to myself from November 2022: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: The problem with benchmarks is usually how novelty can impersonate depth
summary: A personal essay about benchmarks, and the longer argument hidden inside how novelty can impersonate depth.
topic: benchmarks while taste and trust were both up for grabs
body: There was a version of benchmarks circulating in June 2022 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Standing inside a group chat melting down over the same screenshot, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of what keeps this topic interesting is that it resists clean ideological sorting. Benchmarks can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

In 2022, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not just adopting systems; they were auditioning identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted relief from tasks that had become exhausting, repetitive, or plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. It is that you end up building institutions around a feeling, and institutions are much harder to unwind than a hot take in a group chat.

Over time I have learned to pay close attention to what disappears from a story once people start repeating it professionally. Usually it is the same cluster of missing pieces: labor, error handling, emotional load, repair, governance, and the uneven distribution of inconvenience. Benchmarks looked different to me once I framed it that way. What had first appeared as a sleek solution started to read more like a bundle of trade-offs with a persuasive publicist. That does not make the thing useless. It just makes it legible again, which is often the first real service criticism can provide.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It lets me see not only what the technologies were doing, but what I was learning to notice. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Benchmarks sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from June 2022: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: What prompt rituals taught me about building futures

topic: prompt rituals when everyone had a demo but nobody had a shared vocabulary
body: I wrote this down in May 2022 after thinking about prompt rituals from the vantage point of a browser window full of generated images. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. Prompt rituals seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, prompt rituals stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

I kept thinking about an older post of mine, “The problem with tool-using models is usually how novelty can impersonate depth,” from March 2022. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was just incomplete in a way optimism often is when the machinery has not yet touched enough lives.

That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “A note on mutual aid apps after convenience turned into dependency.”

title: On open weights as the internet learned a new kind of performance
summary: A reflection on open weights and the kind of world it quietly assumes.
topic: open weights as the internet learned a new kind of performance
body: My notes on open weights began with a small irritation in May 2022. Everyone around me seemed to agree that the big story was obvious, and the agreement itself made me uneasy. When consensus arrives too quickly, I start looking for whatever got edited out to make the sentence so clean. In this case the omitted piece was the pressure to form a worldview too quickly. That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

Open weights appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Every tool arrives carrying an image of the person who will use it and the society it hopes to stabilize. If you listen carefully, you can hear those assumptions in the design choices. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also what kind of behavior, dependency, and mood the system quietly normalizes while it works.

What I kept seeing was a gap between prototype conditions and public conditions. In the prototype, everyone knows they are participating in an experiment. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. It is another to fold the same logic into schools, offices, hospitals, transit, or housing and expect gratitude because the interface looks modern. The pressure to form a worldview too quickly becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

What changed most was not my taste for the topic but my threshold for abstraction. After writing “The problem with tool-using models is usually how novelty can impersonate depth,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. If a claim about the future cannot survive contact with somebody's day, their tools, their fatigue, and their incentives, I do not trust the claim to survive for long at all. What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. But let us stay attentive to the conditions under which usefulness turns into dependence and strangeness gets marketed as destiny. Open weights seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. More often it is a style of relationship that becomes normal before we have finished deciding whether we like it.

A final note to myself from May 2022: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. title: The problem with coordination systems is usually software stepping into spaces that used to belong to habit
summary: A reflection on coordination systems and the kind of world it quietly assumes.
topic: coordination systems after convenience turned into dependency
body: I wrote this down in November 2021 after thinking about coordination systems from the vantage point of the unnerving quiet of a masked grocery line. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. Coordination systems seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, coordination systems stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

Looking back from November 2021, I could already feel my own position shifting. A few months earlier I had written “What telepresence rituals taught me about building futures,” and the difference between that piece and this one is instructive. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. Here I was more interested in what happens after the applause: the maintenance burden, the political assumptions, the quiet social sorting, the emotional contract being drafted between human beings and the systems now arranging their choices. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was just incomplete in a way optimism often is when the machinery has not yet touched enough lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can admire good tools without surrendering the right to ask what world they are quietly assembling around themselves. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. It begins by refusing the oversized story until the smaller one proves it deserves to grow. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “On maker cities as a rehearsal for a different kind of city.”

A final note to myself from November 2021: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. body: My notes on telepresence rituals began with a small irritation in October 2021. Everyone around me seemed to agree that the big story was obvious, and the agreement itself made me uneasy. In this case the omitted piece was how coordination can feel intimate and brutal at the same time. That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. topic: public health dashboards when the world stopped pretending slack was infinite
body: There was a version of public health dashboards circulating in October 2021 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Standing inside an empty downtown street, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of what keeps this topic interesting is that it resists clean ideological sorting. Public health dashboards can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

In 2021, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not just adopting systems; they were auditioning identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted relief from tasks that had become exhausting, repetitive, or plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. It is that you end up building institutions around a feeling, and institutions are much harder to unwind than a hot take in a group chat.

Over time I have learned to pay close attention to what disappears from a story once people start repeating it professionally. Usually it is the same cluster of missing pieces: labor, error handling, emotional load, repair, governance, and the uneven distribution of inconvenience. Public health dashboards looked different to me once I framed it that way. What had first appeared as a sleek solution started to read more like a bundle of trade-offs with a persuasive publicist. That does not make the thing useless. It just makes it legible again, which is often the first real service criticism can provide.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It lets me see not only what the technologies were doing, but what I was learning to notice. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Public health dashboards sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

When consensus arrives too quickly, I start looking for whatever got edited out to make the sentence so clean. In this case the omitted piece was software stepping into spaces that used to belong to habit. That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

Delivery logistics appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Every tool arrives carrying an image of the person who will use it and the society it hopes to stabilize. If you listen carefully, you can hear those assumptions in the design choices. title: On coordination systems when the world stopped pretending slack was infinite
summary: A reflection on coordination systems and the kind of world it quietly assumes.
topic: coordination systems when the world stopped pretending slack was infinite
body: I wrote this down in February 2021 after thinking about coordination systems from the vantage point of an empty downtown street. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. Coordination systems seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, coordination systems stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

Looking back from February 2021, I could already feel my own position shifting. A few months earlier I had written “On telepresence rituals under pressure,” and the difference between that piece and this one is instructive. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. Here I was more interested in what happens after the applause: the maintenance burden, the political assumptions, the quiet social sorting, the emotional contract being drafted between human beings and the systems now arranging their choices. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was just incomplete in a way optimism often is when the machinery has not yet touched enough lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can admire good tools without surrendering the right to ask what world they are quietly assembling around themselves. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. It begins by refusing the oversized story until the smaller one proves it deserves to grow. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “What communal tools taught me about building futures.”

A final note to myself from February 2021: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: The problem with contactless interfaces is usually the difference between resilience and improvisation
summary: A reflection on contactless interfaces and the kind of world it quietly assumes.
topic: contactless interfaces while daily life was being rerouted through software
body: There was a version of contactless interfaces circulating in June 2020 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Standing inside an empty downtown street, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of what keeps this topic interesting is that it resists clean ideological sorting. Contactless interfaces can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

In 2020, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not just adopting systems; they were auditioning identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted relief from tasks that had become exhausting, repetitive, or plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. It is that you end up building institutions around a feeling, and institutions are much harder to unwind than a hot take in a group chat.

Over time I have learned to pay close attention to what disappears from a story once people start repeating it professionally. Usually it is the same cluster of missing pieces: labor, error handling, emotional load, repair, governance, and the uneven distribution of inconvenience. Contactless interfaces looked different to me once I framed it that way. What had first appeared as a sleek solution started to read more like a bundle of trade-offs with a persuasive publicist. That does not make the thing useless. It just makes it legible again, which is often the first real service criticism can provide.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It lets me see not only what the technologies were doing, but what I was learning to notice. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Contactless interfaces sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from June 2020: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: What homebound automation taught me about building futures
summary: A post about homebound automation when the world stopped pretending slack was infinite, written against the temptation to make the future sound cleaner than it is.
topic: homebound automation when the world stopped pretending slack was infinite
body: I wrote this down in May 2020 after thinking about homebound automation from the vantage point of a video call where everyone looked slightly ghosted by their own webcam. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. Homebound automation seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, homebound automation stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

Looking back from May 2020, I could already feel my own position shifting. I kept thinking about an older post of mine, “The problem with fragile supply chains is usually the difference between resilience and improvisation,” from January 2020. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. Here I was more interested in what happens after the applause: the maintenance burden, the political assumptions, the quiet social sorting, the emotional contract being drafted between human beings and the systems now arranging their choices. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was just incomplete in a way optimism often is when the machinery has not yet touched enough lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can admire good tools without surrendering the right to ask what world they are quietly assembling around themselves. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. It begins by refusing the oversized story until the smaller one proves it deserves to grow. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “A note on human-scale robotics as an art project with civic consequences.”

A final note to myself from May 2020: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: On remote presence under pressure
summary: A personal essay about remote presence, and the longer argument hidden inside software stepping into spaces that used to belong to habit.
topic: remote presence under pressure
body: My notes on remote presence began with a small irritation in April 2020. Everyone around me seemed to agree that the big story was obvious, and the agreement itself made me uneasy. When consensus arrives too quickly, I start looking for whatever got edited out to make the sentence so clean. In this case the omitted piece was software stepping into spaces that used to belong to habit. That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

Remote presence appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Every tool arrives carrying an image of the person who will use it and the society it hopes to stabilize. If you listen carefully, you can hear those assumptions in the design choices. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also what kind of behavior, dependency, and mood the system quietly normalizes while it works.

What I kept seeing was a gap between prototype conditions and public conditions. In the prototype, everyone knows they are participating in an experiment. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. It is another to fold the same logic into schools, offices, hospitals, transit, or housing and expect gratitude because the interface looks modern. Software stepping into spaces that used to belong to habit becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

What changed most was not my taste for the topic but my threshold for abstraction. After writing “The problem with fragile supply chains is usually the difference between resilience and improvisation,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. If a claim about the future cannot survive contact with somebody's day, their tools, their fatigue, and their incentives, I do not trust the claim to survive for long at all. That has become one of my small private tests. Ask the sentence to descend from altitude. If it lands gracefully, keep it. If it panics on impact, start over.

What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. But let us stay attentive to the conditions under which usefulness turns into dependence and strangeness gets marketed as destiny. Remote presence seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. More often it is a style of relationship that becomes normal before we have finished deciding whether we like it.

A final note to myself from April 2020: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: The problem with autonomous transit is usually the way optimism can hide a labor model
summary: A personal essay about autonomous transit, and the longer argument hidden inside the way optimism can hide a labor model.
topic: autonomous transit when everything still felt handmade
body: I wrote this down in August 2019 after thinking about autonomous transit from the vantage point of the desert at sunrise. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. Autonomous transit seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, autonomous transit stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

Looking back from August 2019, I could already feel my own position shifting. A few months earlier I had written “What communal tools taught me about building futures,” and the difference between that piece and this one is instructive. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. Here I was more interested in what happens after the applause: the maintenance burden, the political assumptions, the quiet social sorting, the emotional contract being drafted between human beings and the systems now arranging their choices. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was just incomplete in a way optimism often is when the machinery has not yet touched enough lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can admire good tools without surrendering the right to ask what world they are quietly assembling around themselves. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. It begins by refusing the oversized story until the smaller one proves it deserves to grow. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “I keep coming back to repair culture.”

A final note to myself from August 2019: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. title: On domestic robots as a rehearsal for a different kind of city
summary: A reflection on domestic robots and the kind of world it quietly assumes.
topic: domestic robots as a rehearsal for a different kind of city
body: There was a version of domestic robots circulating in June 2019 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Standing inside an overcaffeinated dinner where everyone had a sketchbook open, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of what keeps this topic interesting is that it resists clean ideological sorting. Domestic robots can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

In 2019, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not just adopting systems; they were auditioning identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted relief from tasks that had become exhausting, repetitive, or plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. It is that you end up building institutions around a feeling, and institutions are much harder to unwind than a hot take in a group chat.

Over time I have learned to pay close attention to what disappears from a story once people start repeating it professionally. Usually it is the same cluster of missing pieces: labor, error handling, emotional load, repair, governance, and the uneven distribution of inconvenience. Domestic robots looked different to me once I framed it that way. What had first appeared as a sleek solution started to read more like a bundle of trade-offs with a persuasive publicist. That does not make the thing useless. It just makes it legible again, which is often the first real service criticism can provide.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It lets me see not only what the technologies were doing, but what I was learning to notice. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Domestic robots sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from June 2019: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

When consensus arrives too quickly, I start looking for whatever got edited out to make the sentence so clean. In this case the omitted piece was the mismatch between prototypes and public life. That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

Tiny labs appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Every tool arrives carrying an image of the person who will use it and the society it hopes to stabilize. If you listen carefully, you can hear those assumptions in the design choices. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also what kind of behavior, dependency, and mood the system quietly normalizes while it works.

What I kept seeing was a gap between prototype conditions and public conditions. In the prototype, everyone knows they are participating in an experiment. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. It is another to fold the same logic into schools, offices, hospitals, transit, or housing and expect gratitude because the interface looks modern. The mismatch between prototypes and public life becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

What changed most was not my taste for the topic but my threshold for abstraction. After writing “In Dust We Trust,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. If a claim about the future cannot survive contact with somebody's day, their tools, their fatigue, and their incentives, I do not trust the claim to survive for long at all. That has become one of my small private tests. Ask the sentence to descend from altitude. If it lands gracefully, keep it. If it panics on impact, start over.

What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. But let us stay attentive to the conditions under which usefulness turns into dependence and strangeness gets marketed as destiny. Tiny labs seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. More often it is a style of relationship that becomes normal before we have finished deciding whether we like it.

A final note to myself from October 2018: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

references.title: In Dust We Trust

title: In Dust We Trust
summary: A first post from Burning Man 2018, where robot fantasies only felt worth keeping if they could survive the dust and still point toward a livable world.
topic: Burning Man 2018, robots, sustainability, and civic imagination
body: I came home from Burning Man 2018 with alkaline dust in my shoes, a sore throat, and one stubborn thought I could not shake: if we are going to let machines organize more of human life, they should at least earn the right to do it in a world that still feels playful. That year the robot theme was impossible to miss. Everywhere you looked there were chrome costumes, handmade exoskeletons, animatronic contraptions, solar creatures wobbling through whiteout conditions, and people talking about automation as if it might become either our servant or our final mirror. The mood was not efficiency. It was theater, curiosity, and a strangely sincere hope that technology could still be intimate rather than managerial.

What I loved about the desert version of robotics was how obviously provisional it all was. A machine broke and five strangers crouched around it with zip ties, a bike light, and some wild theory about torque. Nobody there believed the robot was magic. It was a contraption in a temporary city built by people who knew maintenance was part of the fantasy. That matters to me. The future I want is not one where robots replace us so cleanly that the human part disappears. I want a future where the burden of dull work gets lighter, where energy gets cleaner, where logistics feel less cruel, and where the systems around us are designed with enough humility that ordinary people can still understand the seams.

The harder question is whether that future can stay joyful once it becomes scalable. It is easy to adore a robot when it is dust-covered, solar-powered, and clearly part art project. It is harder when the same logic arrives as surveillance, labor discipline, or a dashboard optimized by someone who has never been hot, tired, and running late. The desert is useful because it exaggerates values. If something is wasteful, flimsy, or fake-generous, the playa reveals it quickly. Sustainability is not a slogan out there. You feel it in water, battery life, shade, repair, and whether the thing you built can survive contact with wind and exhaustion. I wish more of mainstream technology had to pass through that kind of honesty.

My optimistic version of robotics still looks weirdly simple. Let the machines do the repetitive hauling, the dangerous inspection, the midnight sorting, the ugly heat. Let them make cities quieter, cleaner, and easier to share. Let them help us produce abundance without producing the usual giant pile of waste. But if they are going to run more of the world, then the world they run should be worth inheriting. It should be sustainable first, legible second, and beautiful almost by accident. It should not feel like we traded one extractive system for a more frictionless one. I do not want the smart city as a mall with sensors. I want the smart city as a place where people have more time, less drudgery, and more reasons to trust each other.

The phrase that kept circling in my head all week was in dust we trust. Not because dust is noble, but because it is democratic. It gets on everything. It levels expensive and handmade objects alike. It reminds you that no machine is above its environment. That may be the principle I want to keep. Build robots, yes. Build software, sure. But build them in a way that admits they live among bodies, weather, repair cycles, limits, and actual communities. If a machine cannot survive a little dust without becoming authoritarian about it, I am not convinced it belongs in charge of much.

So that is where I am starting this blog: not with a polished thesis, but with a scene. A temporary city. Robot costumes at sunset. Solar chargers humming like a promise. A lot of very smart people still acting as if delight matters. Maybe that is naive. Maybe a few years from now I will read this back and find it impossibly earnest. But I would rather begin with the possibility that technology could serve a livable world than begin with cynicism and call it maturity. If the future is going to be automated, I want to keep asking the same question until it becomes annoying: automated toward what kind of life?
